title: SEGMENT 16 — Fish for cancer: transgenic models of melanoma, leukemia, and retinoblastoma

lines.text: SEGMENT 16 — Fish for cancer: transgenic models of melanoma, leukemia, and retinoblastoma
One application of zebrafish has grown enormously over the past twenty years: cancer modeling.
The idea is straightforward — take a human oncogene, put it into a fish under a tissue-specific promoter, and watch the fish develop a cancer of that tissue.

Because the fish is transparent at embryonic and larval stages and partially transparent as an adult (especially in pigment-mutant strains like casper, which lack body pigment), you can watch tumors form, grow, invade, and metastasize in a live vertebrate, in real time, under a microscope.
A few specific examples:
Melanoma.
Leonard Zon's lab at Harvard made a transgenic zebrafish carrying the human oncogenic BRAF-V600E mutation under control of a melanocyte-specific promoter.
The fish develop melanoma.
Crossed with a p53 mutant background, they develop melanoma rapidly and aggressively.
The model has been used to identify modifier genes that accelerate or suppress melanoma — including genes that have led to new therapeutic candidates being tested in human melanoma trials.
Leukemia.

Transgenic fish expressing the human oncofusion MYC-ER in lymphocyte precursors develop T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia, which can be tracked by GFP-labeling the leukemic cells and watching them spread through the live fish.
Retinoblastoma and other pediatric tumors.

Fish models have been built for retinoblastoma, neuroblastoma, rhabdomyosarcoma, and several other rare pediatric cancers — diseases for which mouse models exist but are expensive enough that screening large numbers of candidate genes is impractical.
The fish enables faster modifier screens.
The advantage of fish cancer models over mouse models is throughput and visibility.
You can screen thousands of fish for drug efficacy in a way you can't screen thousands of mice.
You can watch a single tumor grow over weeks in a live animal, taking daily fluorescence images.

The disadvantages: fish lack adaptive immunity exactly as it works in mammals, fish don't have lungs (a common metastasis site in human cancer), and some fish biology — pigmentation, fin regeneration, scale biology — has no human equivalent.
The model is partial.
But for the questions it's good at — early tumor initiation, modifier genetics, drug screening — it's increasingly important.
The big idea: fish are now a serious cancer modeling platform, particularly when speed, visibility, and modifier screening matter more than perfect mammalian fidelity.
The mouse remains the gold standard for translational cancer biology, but the fish covers the questions the mouse can't address at scale.
